text: The surgeon identifies an incarcerated right indirect inguinal hernia and begins dissection at the medial umbilical region.

The surgeon works from the supravesicular space, moving medially to laterally. Initially, the dissection goes slightly deeper than intended, but the surgeon adjusts to stay more superficial within the plane.
The surgeon works laterally towards Bogros' space, pushing the fat laterally. The peritoneal flap becomes less visible, prompting the surgeon to reorient to maintain a thin flap.

The surgeon performs a lateral dissection near the flap for an indirect inguinal hernia, ensuring the mesh will lay flat. The dissection extends from medial to lateral and back, continuing the supravesicular dissection.
The surgeon continues dissection from lateral to medial and vice versa, staying close to the peritoneal flap and progressing towards the deep internal ring.
The surgeon performs a straightforward dissection, taking the tissue down to Cooper's ligament and extending the flap far below it.

The surgeon encounters a sticky area along the iliopubic tract at the medial boundary of the deep internal ring. The hernia sac is visible extending into the neck of the indirect inguinal hernia. The surgeon switches to an oblique angle, using gentle cautery and sweeping motions to stay close to the hernia sac while pulling it up and to the left.

The surgeon encounters a large cord lipoma while dissecting, pulling on the hernia sac to reveal the spermatic cord and vas deferens. Gentle electrocautery is used to sweep the tissue, allowing the spermatic cord to fall away and the vas deferens to become visible.

The surgeon performs a dissection maneuver referred to as 'coming around the corner,' working laterally to safely identify the spermatic cord. The hernia sac is gently swept down off the spermatic cord, revealing the vas deferens. Tissue above and medial to the vas is visible and can be taken.
The surgeon identifies the main branch of the vein off the chronomortis, ensuring it is clean before placing the most medial stitch on Cooper's ligament, just medial to the vein.
The surgeon creates a deep pocket in the surgical field to accommodate an extra-large mesh implant.
The surgeon continues dissection around the corner, expanding the pocket for an extra large mesh. The surgical field shows no evidence of a cord lipoma.
The surgeon sweeps tissue off the Cooper's ligament, ensuring no evidence of a recurrent direct space hernia. The vein is visible to the left, while the iliac vessels are positioned to the right.

The surgeon addresses a typical right indirect inguinal hernia, focusing on high medial fixation. The area is inspected for a clear landing path for the needle, with caution due to potential bleeding risks observed in previous cases.
The surgeon performs a running canal baseball stitch using a 2-0 V-lock suture, which is visually absorbed into the surrounding tissue, effectively burying the stitch.
The surgeon placates the indirect angle hernia sac, suturing it back onto the flap to prevent recurrence and avoid potential bowel obstructions caused by the hernia sac.

The surgeon inspects for holes or defects in the hernia sac, then pulls and sweeps the sac upwards, suturing it onto the flap laterally. The large inguinal scrotal hernia sac is draped over the suture line, extending laterally.
